text: The go-to operation in case of a peristomal hernia for most hernia surgeons will be a laparoscopic sugar baker these days. The laparoscopic sugar baker is known to have a recurrence rate of approximately ten to fifteen percent. A relatively new technique for the treatment of a primary or recurrent peristomal hernia is the retromuscular mesh technique as described by Pauli and in this case it is performed robotically. This patient underwent a laparoscopic abdominal perineal resection for severe fecal incontinence in twenty nineteen and developed a peristomal hernia quite early on afterwards which was repaired by a laparoscopic sugar baker repair in the summer of twenty twenty. She was referred to me after a recurrence occurred after about nine months after the primary operation.

Discussing the development of the recurrence in this patient, two things can be remarked. The first thing is the use of resorbable tackers, which have been shown to lead to a higher recurrence rate than non-absorbable tackers. The second aspect is the lateralization of the stoma conduit, which is one of the most important technical aspects of the sugar baker or the poly repair. And in this case, it seems that the lateralization, so the lateral coverage of the mesh is inadequate. In principle, the technique of the robotic poly repair is not different in a recurrence than in a primary peristomal hernia repair. One of the main issues of course is that the reduction of the hernia itself and the hernia sac will be hampered by the presence of the mesh and the ingrowth of the mesh into the bowel itself will make the dissection of the plane slightly more difficult which we will see along in this video.

Following adhesiolysis and reduction of the hernia sac, the surgeon begins dissection into the preperitoneal plane at the midline. The presence of a concomitant midline hernia necessitates adjustment of the technique to include a TAROP operation. Dissection starts in the contralateral rectomuscular plane to ensure sufficient mesh overlap after midline hernia closure. The preperitoneal plane dissection is challenged by existing mesh and tackers, but progresses into the retromuscular plane, where dissection is less impeded by the IPOM mesh or absorbable tackers.

The surgeon encounters difficulty while dissecting the retromuscular plane surrounding the stoma due to mesh ingrowth, which poses a risk of bowel injury. As the dissection progresses into the lower abdomen, the arcuate line is crossed, and the absence of posterior rectus fascia is noted. The dissection continues in the preperitoneal plane, with mesh ingrowth again complicating the process. The recto-rectus plane is developed and divided towards the stoma, allowing the flaps to hang downwards for better access. The mesh is visibly adhered to the bowel, making dissection challenging, and part of the mesh is left attached to the bowel to avoid potential damage.

The surgeon performs a release of the transverse abdominis muscle to achieve further lateralization of the stoma. The dissection continues laterally in the preperitoneal plane, ventral to the transversalis fascia, and caudally to the stoma to ensure proper overlap. Sutures are used for lateralization of the stoma in the robotic poly repair.

The surgeon closes the defect using a zero V-lock continuous suture. A cut-to-size polypropylene mesh is placed over the defect and fixed with sutures loosely to prevent mesh erosion. The posterior layer is closed using an IIO V-lock suture.
